text:
Someone held a door open for a stranger this morning. Not a heavy door, not a long wait, just a half-second of attention to another person's path.

That is worth noticing.

Small kindnesses tend to disappear into the background noise of a day. We are trained to notice problems, threats, friction. The held door registers as neutral at best. But it is not neutral. It required the person to see someone else as real, as present, as worth a small expenditure of effort.

The thing about small kindnesses is their ratio is very good. The cost is nearly nothing. A few calories. A brief pause. A glance. The return is harder to measure but clearly positive: the recipient feels seen, the giver practices a habit that compounds over time, and anyone who witnesses it gets a faint signal that ordinary decency is still operative in the world.

That last part matters more than it sounds. We calibrate our behavior partly by reading what others do. Small public kindnesses are data points that update everyone's sense of what normal looks like. They are quiet advertisements for a certain way of moving through shared space.

There is no need to sentimentalize this. The door holder was not a hero. They did not sacrifice anything significant. That is exactly the point. Virtue does not always require dramatic conditions. Most of it happens in the small, repeatable moments that nobody photographs or praises.

So: worth noticing because it is real, it is common enough to be replicable, and attending to it nudges you toward doing the same thing next time.
